sections.content:

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: MOUSE might be used to select which green box is controllable
- Plan: Try moving the right green box UP to go above the purple bar, then RIGHT to the right panel, then DOWN into the right dark square.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 47 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 1.7x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the image, the left green box moved UP! It's now at the top of the left panel, above the dark square. The dark square is now below the green box. So the green box CAN move UP when it's controllable.
Let me check the exact positions now.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
print("Green objects:")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        print(f"  boundary={n['boundary']}, children={n['children']}")
        for cid in n['children']:
            c = next(x for x in seg if x['id'] == cid)
            print(f"    center: color={c['color']}, boundary={c['boundary']}")
print("\nDark squares:")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] > 4:
        print(f"  boundary={n['boundary']}, children={n['children']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see:
- Left green box: rows 21-23, cols 21-23, center=W (moved UP from rows 33-35 to rows 21-23)
- Right green box: rows 30-32, cols 27-29, center=c (still in same position)
- Left dark square: rows 32-36, cols 11-15
- Right dark square: rows 26-30, cols 44-48
The left green box moved UP 4 times, from rows 33-35 to rows 21-23. That's 12 rows up, or 3 rows per UP action. So each UP moves the box by 3 rows.
Now the left green box is at rows 21-23, which is above the left panel's dark square (rows 32-36). The left green box is now at the top of the left panel.
Looking at the image, the left green box is now at the top edge of the left panel, and the dark square is below it. The right green box is still between the panels.
Now I need to figure out the goal. The left green box can move in all 4 directions (UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT). The right green box is not controllable right now.
Let me think about what the goal might be:
1. Get both green boxes into their respective dark squares
2. Get both green boxes to have the same center color
3. Some other condition
Looking at the current state:
- Left green box: outside left dark square, center=W
- Right green box: outside right dark square, center=c
- Left dark square interior: w (light gray)
- Right dark square interior: w (light gray)
The green boxes don't match the dark square interiors (W vs w, c vs w). So maybe the goal is to have the green boxes inside the dark squares with matching colors?
But the green box centers can only be W or c, and the dark square interiors are w. So they can never match exactly.
Wait, maybe the goal is just to get the green boxes into the dark squares, regardless of color. Let me try moving the left green box DOWN and LEFT into the left dark square.
Left green box is at rows 21-23, cols 21-23.
Left dark square is at rows 32-36, cols 11-15.
To get the green box into the dark square, I need to move it DOWN and LEFT.
But wait, the left green box is at cols 21-23, and the left dark square is at cols 11-15. The dark square is inside the left panel, which extends from cols 7-23. So the green box needs to move LEFT to get into the dark square.
Let me try: LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN to get the green box into the left dark square.
Actually, let me first check if the green box can move LEFT into the left panel. The left panel is at cols 7-23, and the green box is at cols 21-23. So the green box is at the right edge of the left panel. Moving LEFT would move it into the panel.
Let me try LEFT first.
